content.text: Definition of Done for Claude Feature Delivery

A 'Definition of Done' (DoD) is a shared, explicit checklist that every Claude feature must satisfy before it is considered complete and shippable. Without a DoD, 'done' means whatever the author thinks it means at the time — which is usually 'the happy path works in dev'. A shared DoD makes implicit quality expectations explicit and gives reviewers, PMs, and on-call engineers a single source of truth for what was verified before a feature shipped.

attrs.alt: Six-stage DoD pipeline: Code Complete, Prompt Reviewed, Observability Verified, Security Cleared, Staging Validated, Done/Shippable

content.text: Why Claude Features Need a Specialised DoD
Standard software DoD checklists cover code review, tests, and deployment. Claude features require additional gates because:
Model behaviour is non-deterministic — a feature can pass unit tests in CI and still produce harmful or incorrect output for users depending on prompt wording, model version, or input distribution.
Prompt templates are versioned artifacts with their own review and approval lifecycle, separate from code review.
Token usage and cost are operational concerns that traditional software does not have; a feature that works correctly but costs 10× more than projected is not shippable.
Safety and injection resistance must be verified before production exposure, not discovered from user reports.
Observability requirements are higher: token usage, model ID, stop_reason, and latency must all be recorded to diagnose production issues.
The Six DoD Gates
Gate 1 — Code Complete
All implementation code is merged to the main branch, passes CI, and has test coverage meeting the project threshold:
Unit tests mock at the SDK boundary — no real API calls in CI.
Rate-limit path (HTTP 429) is covered by at least one test.
Truncation path (stop_reason = max_tokens) is covered by at least one test.
Injection scenario test is present in the test suite.
Type annotations are complete and mypy/pyright passes with zero errors.
No TODO or FIXME comments related to the Claude integration remain in the merged code.
Gate 2 — Prompt Reviewed and Approved
All prompt templates touched by this feature have completed the full review and approval process:
Template YAML schema validation passed (Gate 1 in the prompt review pipeline).
Injection resistance probes passed (Gate 2 in the prompt review pipeline).
Regression eval pass rate meets or exceeds the configured minimum (Gate 3 in the prompt review pipeline).
Prompt lead has approved the template PR.
Security reviewer has approved if the template is customer-facing or uses tool calls.
Template version is bumped according to the change tier (patch / minor / major).
Changelog entry for the new version is present in the template YAML.
Gate 3 — Observability Verified
Every API call the feature makes emits the required structured telemetry:

# Acceptance test: call the feature once and assert the telemetry is present
import structlog, json
from io import StringIO
import pytest

def test_telemetry_emitted(capfd, monkeypatch):
    """Every Claude API call must produce a structured log with required fields."""
    logs = []
    # Capture structlog JSON output
    import logging
    handler = logging.StreamHandler(stream := StringIO())
    logging.getLogger().addHandler(handler)

    # Exercise the feature
    result = my_claude_feature(user_input="What is on my invoice?")

    # Parse logs
    stream.seek(0)
    for line in stream.getvalue().splitlines():
        try:
            logs.append(json.loads(line))
        except json.JSONDecodeError:
            pass

    claude_log = next(
        (l for l in logs if l.get("event") == "claude_api_call"),
        None,
    )
    assert claude_log is not None, "No claude_api_call log found"

    required_fields = {"model_id", "feature", "team", "input_tokens",
                       "output_tokens", "latency_ms", "stop_reason"}
    missing = required_fields - claude_log.keys()
    assert not missing, f"Missing telemetry fields: {missing}"
OTel span wrapping the API call is present with model_id, latency, and token attributes.
Prometheus counter and histogram are updated on every call with feature and team labels.
Cost attribution (team, feature, cost_usd) is recorded per call.
Audit log entry is emitted for any call that processes personal data.
Gate 4 — Security Cleared
The security reviewer has confirmed that the feature meets the security baseline:
User-supplied content is never interpolated into the system prompt.
All context injected into the user or system turn is wrapped in namespace-qualified delimiters.
PII in model inputs is stripped or hashed before being written to any log.
Model output is not written to unencrypted storage.
If the feature uses tool calls, each tool's input schema limits the scope to the minimum required.
Output scanner is configured if the feature serves content to end users.
Gate 5 — Staging Validated
The feature has been deployed to the staging environment and the eval suite has been run against it end-to-end (not just against the prompt template in isolation):
Validation check
Pass condition
Who verifies
Eval suite pass rate (staging)
≥ configured min_pass_rate for the template
Release pipeline (automated)
Latency P95
≤ SLA target for the endpoint (typically 3s for synchronous, 30s for streaming)
Release pipeline (automated)
Cost per request (staging)
Within 20% of the projected cost from the template's token budget estimate
Release pipeline (automated)
Error rate (staging)
< 1% over 500 sample requests
Release pipeline (automated)
PM acceptance test
PM or product owner confirms the feature behaves correctly on their defined acceptance scenarios
PM (manual)
Gate 6 — Done / Shippable

After all five gates pass, the PM or team lead marks the feature as Done. Done means it is eligible for canary deployment to production — not that it is fully live. The canary deployment is a separate release process step, not part of the DoD.

A feature marked Done must remain shippable at any point. If a regression is found after marking Done, the feature reverts to in-progress and re-enters the DoD pipeline at the gate where the regression was detected.
DoD Checklist in Practice
Teams typically track DoD progress as a checklist on the GitHub issue or Jira ticket. A template that can be pasted into any Claude feature ticket:

## Definition of Done — Claude Feature

### Gate 1: Code Complete
- [ ] All code merged to main; CI green
- [ ] Unit tests mock at SDK boundary (no real API calls in CI)
- [ ] Rate-limit path (429) covered by test
- [ ] Truncation path (max_tokens stop_reason) covered by test
- [ ] Injection scenario test present
- [ ] mypy / pyright passes with zero errors

### Gate 2: Prompt Reviewed
- [ ] Prompt schema validation CI check: passed
- [ ] Injection resistance CI check: passed
- [ ] Regression eval CI check: passed (pass rate ≥ min_pass_rate)
- [ ] Prompt lead approval: @___
- [ ] Security reviewer approval (if customer-facing or tool-use): @___
- [ ] Version bumped; changelog entry added

### Gate 3: Observability Verified
- [ ] structlog JSON log with required fields on every API call
- [ ] OTel span present with model_id, latency, tokens
- [ ] Prometheus counter/histogram with feature + team labels
- [ ] Audit log for PII-touching calls

### Gate 4: Security Cleared
- [ ] User input not in system prompt
- [ ] Namespace delimiters on all injected context
- [ ] PII stripped/hashed before logging
- [ ] Output not in unencrypted storage
- [ ] Tool call scope minimal (if applicable)
- [ ] Output scanner configured (if user-facing)
- [ ] Security reviewer sign-off: @___

### Gate 5: Staging Validated
- [ ] Eval suite pass rate ≥ min_pass_rate in staging
- [ ] P95 latency ≤ SLA target
- [ ] Cost per request within 20% of projection
- [ ] Error rate < 1% over 500 requests
- [ ] PM acceptance test: passed

### Gate 6: Done
- [ ] PM or team lead sign-off: @___
- [ ] Feature eligible for canary deployment
Common DoD Failures and Their Causes
Gate
Common failure
Root cause
Prevention
1 — Code
Injection test missing
Developer did not know injection tests were required; standard code review did not catch it
Include injection test as a blocking checklist item in PR template; add to code review checklist
2 — Prompt
Prompt PR merged without security reviewer
CODEOWNERS not configured for the template's directory; prompt lead approved without noticing the template was customer-facing
Audit CODEOWNERS on each new template directory; prompt lead checklist item: confirm the correct CODEOWNERS entry exists
3 — Observability
Token usage not recorded
Developer used a wrapper library that silently dropped usage from the response
Observability acceptance test (as above) run in staging blocks promotion if fields are missing
4 — Security
PII in log field
Developer logged the full request object during debugging and forgot to remove it
Semgrep rule blocking raw message logging; security reviewer spot-checks logs in staging
5 — Staging
Eval pass rate below threshold
Prompt regression from unrelated change; golden dataset not updated after product change expanded input space
Gate 3 of the prompt review pipeline catches regressions before staging; staging eval is the second checkpoint
Automating DoD Gate Tracking
GitHub issue automation

Manually ticking checkboxes is error-prone under release pressure. A GitHub Actions workflow can automatically check off DoD items when the corresponding CI gate passes or a required approval is recorded:

# scripts/update-dod-checklist.py
# Called by CI to mark DoD items complete on the linked issue.
import os, re, requests

GITHUB_TOKEN = os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"]
REPO = os.environ["GITHUB_REPOSITORY"]
ISSUE_NUMBER = int(os.environ["DOD_ISSUE_NUMBER"])

GATE_MARKERS = {
    "gate_1_schema": "Prompt schema validation CI check: passed",
    "gate_2_injection": "Injection resistance CI check: passed",
    "gate_3_regression": "Regression eval CI check: passed",
}

def get_issue_body() -> str:
    resp = requests.get(
        f"https://api.github.com/repos/{REPO}/issues/{ISSUE_NUMBER}",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {GITHUB_TOKEN}"},
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()["body"]

def check_off(body: str, marker: str) -> str:
    """Replace '- [ ] <marker>' with '- [x] <marker>'."""
    return re.sub(
        rf"- \[ \] ({re.escape(marker)})",
        r"- [x] \1",
        body,
    )

def update_issue(body: str) -> None:
    requests.patch(
        f"https://api.github.com/repos/{REPO}/issues/{ISSUE_NUMBER}",
        json={"body": body},
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {GITHUB_TOKEN}"},
    ).raise_for_status()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    gate = os.environ["GATE_KEY"]  # e.g. "gate_1_schema"
    marker = GATE_MARKERS[gate]
    body = get_issue_body()
    updated = check_off(body, marker)
    if updated != body:
        update_issue(updated)
        print(f"Checked off: {marker}")
    else:
        print(f"Marker already checked or not found: {marker}")
DoD status badge
A lightweight status endpoint can expose the current DoD gate state as a machine-readable JSON response, enabling dashboards to show which features are blocked at which gate across the sprint:

# app/dod_status.py — FastAPI endpoint (internal tooling)
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
from github import Github  # PyGithub
import re

app = FastAPI()
gh = Github(os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"])

class GateStatus(BaseModel):
    gate: str
    complete: bool
    label: str

class DoDStatus(BaseModel):
    issue_number: int
    feature: str
    gates: list[GateStatus]
    all_done: bool

@app.get("/dod/{issue_number}", response_model=DoDStatus)
def get_dod_status(issue_number: int):
    repo = gh.get_repo(os.environ["GITHUB_REPOSITORY"])
    issue = repo.get_issue(issue_number)
    body = issue.body or ""

    gates = []
    for label, pattern in [
        ("Code Complete", "All code merged to main; CI green"),
        ("Prompt Reviewed", "Prompt lead approval"),
        ("Observability Verified", "OTel span present"),
        ("Security Cleared", "Security reviewer sign-off"),
        ("Staging Validated", "PM acceptance test: passed"),
        ("Done", "PM or team lead sign-off"),
    ]:
        checked = bool(re.search(rf"- \[x\].*{re.escape(pattern)}", body, re.IGNORECASE))
        gates.append(GateStatus(gate=label, complete=checked, label=label))

    return DoDStatus(
        issue_number=issue_number,
        feature=issue.title,
        gates=gates,
        all_done=all(g.complete for g in gates),
    )
DoD Across Different Feature Sizes
Not every Claude feature has the same footprint. The DoD gates are the same regardless of size, but the evidence required at each gate scales with the change:
Feature size
Example
Gate 1 evidence
Gate 2 evidence
Gate 5 evidence
Hotfix (patch)
Fix a typo in a DO NOT instruction that causes unexpected refusals
Single commit, existing tests still green
Patch version bump; schema + injection gates pass
50-request smoke test in staging; existing eval suite unchanged
Small feature
Add a new output format option to an existing prompt
New unit tests for the format option
Minor version bump; all three CI gates pass; prompt lead approval
Full eval suite in staging; PM spot-checks 5 sample outputs
Medium feature
New Claude-powered endpoint with its own system prompt
Full test suite including rate-limit and truncation paths
New template PR with all four prompt review gates; security reviewer approval
End-to-end eval suite with ≥100 golden cases; latency + cost verified
Large feature (new integration)
Multi-step agent workflow with tool calls and PII access
Integration test suite; CDC tests for dependent services
New template per agent step; security reviewer + data-privacy reviewer approval
Load test in staging; canary smoke in production (5% traffic, 30 min)
Runbook: Feature Fails Staging Gate After Done Was Declared

Production incidents occasionally reveal that a feature considered Done contained a defect not caught by the staging eval. The runbook below determines the correct recovery path without discarding work unnecessarily:
Immediately:

check whether the defect is in production. If yes, assess whether a rollback is required. If the defect is limited to staging or was discovered during load testing before canary, no rollback is needed.
Root cause triage:
identify which DoD gate should have caught the defect. Was the eval suite missing a case? Was the security check incomplete? Was PII logging not caught by the automated check?
Revert Done status:
update the feature ticket back to 'in progress' and note which gate failed and why.
Fix the defect and the process gap:
fix the code or prompt and add the missing test / eval case / security check so the same category of defect cannot pass the gate again.
Re-run the affected gate:
do not skip re-running; the purpose is to confirm the fix works under the same conditions that caught the original defect.
Re-mark Done:
only after the affected gate passes again and the process gap is addressed.
Write a brief blameless post-mortem:
record which gate failed, why it was not caught, and what was changed to prevent recurrence. Append it to the feature ticket for future reference.
Summary

The Definition of Done for Claude feature delivery extends the standard software DoD with five Claude-specific gates: prompt reviewed (template passed CI gates and human approval), observability verified (all telemetry fields present), security cleared (injection resistance, PII handling, output safety), and staging validated (eval pass rate, latency, cost). Together with standard code completeness, these six gates ensure a feature is genuinely shippable rather than just 'working on my machine'.
The DoD is a checklist on the feature ticket, not a document — each item is an explicit binary that can be verified independently.
Gates are ordered by when the blocker can be detected: code issues in CI, prompt issues in review, observability and security issues in staging.
Marking Done makes a feature eligible for canary deployment; it does not deploy automatically.
A post-Done regression reverts the feature to in-progress at the gate where the regression was found.
Tracking DoD compliance across features reveals which gates fail most often, directing process improvement effort.
